I WILL GO BACK.

I will go back to my futher’s house and live on my father’s land—
For my father’s house is by Rosses’ shore that slopes to Dooran strand,
And the wild mountains of Donegal rise up on either hand.

I have been gone from Donegal for seven years and a day,
And true encugh it’s a long, long while for a wanderer to stay—
But the hills of home are aye in my heart and never far away.

The long white road winds o’er the hill from Fanad to Kilear,
And slopes apast Gweebara Bay where the deep seca-waters are—
Where the long grey boats go out by night to fish beyond the bar.

Ill lie by the beach the livelong day, where the foreshoro slopes to
the sea—

When the sun is red on the golden, gorse as once it used to be;

And, Ol but it’s many an olden thought will come up in the heart of me.

For the friends of my youth shall gather around, the friends that I
knew of old,

The olden songs will be sung to me and the old, old stories told

Beside the fire of my father’s house when the nights are long and cold.

"Tis there that T’l] pass my years away, back in my native land;

In my father’'s house by Rosses’ shore that lies by Dooran strand,

Where the bills of ancient Donegal rise up on either hand.
PATRICK MacGILL.

THE FRANCISCANS AND THEIR LIBRARY.

On the death of Fr. Hugh Ward (1635) the duty fell on Fr. John
Colzan of arranging and preparing for publication the collection made
by Ward, as well ag that which was being collected and sent over by
Brother Michael from Ircland to Louvain. As Colgan’s life is not
¢asily available a somewhat fuller notice of his career, principally

taken from his life by the Very Rev. Jas. McLaughlin, P.P. o> 10 the
‘“ Frauciscan Tertiary,” 1892-3, may not prove uninteresting, especially
when 1t is remembered the great space his labours fill in our history,
his writings being practically the storehouse for all later historicy)
writers on Ireland.

John Colgan was born in the year 1592, in the townland of Mug
(afterwards called Priestown from the number of priests born therejy
bearing his name) in the parieh of Donaghmore, barony of Innish Owen,
and Diocese of Derry. The parish was an epircopal see in ancient
times, and, in a8 note on the tripartite ife of St. Patrick in his “ Act
Sanctorum,’”” Colgan states that he himeelf was born on the lands of
the cathedral church. No trace of such church now exists, but in the
belfry of the emall Protestant church, built on a part of the site of
the cathedral, one of the old bells is stlll in use, which bears the D-
scription ‘‘ Sancta Maria, ora pro nobis.

Father Colgan was highly skilled in the Irish language, as his con-
temporary, the famous Luke Wadding, states in his Scriptores
Ordinis Minorum,” although O’Curry, 1n his ‘‘ MSS. Materials,”’ would
seem to be under a different impression. Colgan wag ordained priest in
1618, and made his profession in the Franciscan Order two years later.
He studied theology under Fr. Thomas Fleming, third son of the 16th
Baron of Slane, who was afterwards appointed A'r'ch‘bishdp of Dublin.

From the collections made by Fr. Hugh Ward, Brother Michzl,
and by himself; Colgan compiled his ‘‘ Acta Sanctorum,’”’ or Lives of
the Irish Saints, with tho record of their monasteries. This work he
intended to have completed in' four vols., and the first vol. conta 1ning
the lives of the Irish saints whose festivals fall on the dates from
the first January to 3lst March was published in 1645. The expenso
of its publication was borne by the Primate, Hugh O’Reilly. The
second vol. was completed before Colgan’s death, in 1658, but was
never published, and is probably lost. At Louvain Colgan published
the Tria Thaumaturga in 1647, containing the lives of the three patron
saints of Ireland—Patrick, Brigid and Columcille, the ex penses of
which were met by Archbishop Fleming, of Dublin, who, as already
mentioned, was Colgan’s teacher and life-long friond. The most jm-
portant of the lives of St. Patrick used by Colgan in compiling the
Tria Thaumaturga was the ““tripartite life’’ writtem by St. Evin,
which is so called because 1t is divided into three parts——-tho first part
being am account of his parentage, education, bondage, etc,. the

second part his journey to Connaught and his return to Ulster after

a space of seven yeatt:; and the third his mission through Leinster and
Munster with his return to Armagh and death. In Colgan’s time there
were three copies of the tripartite life extant and an 1mperfect copy
i1 the Book of Armagh. Some time cubsequently however to the pub-
lication of the Tria Thaumaturga these copies of the tripartite life
disappeared, until Eugene O’Curry, on a chance visit to the DBritish
Museum 1n May, 1819, diccovered among the MSS. there a copy of the
tripartite Iife, see page 345 of his ‘“ MSS. Materials”’ for a graphic
description of his lucky find. Colgan also publiched in 1655, at Ant-
werp, & life of the iliustrious Irish Philosopher and Franciscan Friar,
Durs Scotus, “‘the Subtle Doctor,” as he was named, and dedicated
it to Colonel O'Reilly, the hero of Benburh and a relative of the
Primate. When one considers that while Colgan was engaged on these
works he was also discharging the laborious duties of Professor of
Theology and Guardian of his Convent, as well as being a man of frail
health, it seemys surprising how he could get through so much work.
In Colgan’s cell, after his death, a catalogue (now in Library, Mer-
chant’s Quay) was found of the MSS. used by him in his work.

The collections of Irish MSS. made by Colean, O’Clery, Ward, and
others were kept in Louvain, but owing to the troubles caused by the
French Revolution, the collections: got dispersed, some finding their
way to the Royal Library at Brussells and elsewhere, but a consider-
able number were preserved by the Franciscans and brought for safety
to Rome and lodged in their college, San Isidore, in that city. . In

1872 the Franciscans Lrought their collection from Rome to Dublin,

where a suitable building hag been provided for their reception. Un-
fortunately many valuable MSS., both in parchment and paper, chiefly
the writings of John Celgan, were jrretrievably lost. Of the three
vols, of Colgan’s unpublished works (which! were described as extant
a4t Louvain -in 1745) enumerating the labours and the monasteries
founded by Trish migsionaries outside Ireland, only a few leaves now
remain, Colgan died 1n his college at Louvain on the 15th January,
1658, as ig evidenced: from a copy of the printed obituary notice pre-
served at Merchants’ Quay.

“In the *“ Rise and Fall of the Trish Franciscan Monatteries,” by the
Rev, ¢, p. Meehan, page 255, the following MSS. are mentioned as
being in the Library, Merchants’ Quay, viz.: Liber Hymnorum (which
bolonged ¢ Donegal Convent Library); part of Pealter in the hand-
iting of St Camin of Inishcaltra (the psalm Beati, No. 118, of the
Vulgate 1q, tion, which was in great use among the early Irish monks

The Library also possesses a voluminous collection of the COITes-
pondence, etc., of Fr. Luke Wadding, who was born in 1588 in Water.
ford, was the founder and the first Guardian of San Isidore (1625), and
also one of the most distinguished Irishmen who went out from Ireland,
He compiled the ‘“ Anmals of the Order of Franciscan Minois,”  two
vols. of which were published in 1629, Lives of the Pontiffs and Car-
dinals, and) the Works of Duns Scotus (1639), notwithstanding his en-
grossing duty as Agent for the Confederate Catholics and the exten-
sive correspondence it entailed. The selections from this correspond-
ence, which was summarised in the Report presented on the MSS. col-
lection, comprise 274 printed pages, and the FEditor remarks that
Wadding brought to his duties ‘‘ not only the learning and acumen
and unflagging energy of a great schoolman, but a lofty patrioticm
tempered by practical sagacity which made him for more than a quarter
of a century the trusted spokesman of the Irish nation at the Roman
Curia.”

PRINTING IN YOUGHAL.

The following list shows the earliest items of printing, and the
printers’ names, ro far as I know them, in the above town down to
1826. They are few in number, but very interesting and rare. No
such list has previously appeared, and 1t m ay perhaps draw attention
to the subject and lead to further titles bein g supplied by our readers.

Archdeacon Cobton gives no authority or reference for his statement

that a printer named Cox was-at work in Youghal in 1770; but Cotton
was too careful and experienced to make such a statement without
reliable information. Thero were sevoral * Li ndsays’’ printers, some

in. Mallow, others in Fermoy, but all probably of the same family or

stock.

CENTENARY OF FATHER MERHAN.

Another centenary, worthy of mote in a magazine devoted to Irish
hiterature, occurred this year, viz., that of Rev. Chas. Patrick Mee-
han, poet, priest and hiktorian, who was born at 141, Great Britain
(now Parnell) Street, Dublin, on 12th July, 1812. His parents had
come from Ballymahon, Co. Longford, a place assdeiated withi the
name of Oliver Goldsmith, and thithen the child was sent for his early
education, At the ago of sixteen he entered the Irish College at Rome,
where ho remained six yoars, perfecting himself in the Italian language
and literature 1n addition to his theological| studies. It was here that
hig higtorice sense was awakened by discovering in the Franciscan church
at Montonio two dilapidated tombstones bearing the names ‘‘ O’Neill,”’
“O0'Donel,” and * Dungannon,’”’ of whose occupants he could gain no
information, Thig discovery was a generation later, after much trouble
?1'1‘1(1' research, to result in the compilation of his best known book.
On concluding hig course of study he was ordained in 1834, and, re-
Wrning to hig native land became ourate of Rathdrum. He was shont-
ly afterwards transferred to Dublin, where the remainder of his long
and usefu] life wag spent in the parish of SS. Michael and John, where
he had ag oolleague a kindred spirit in John, Canon O.Hanlon. Cap-

Lished with valuable notes ‘‘ The Life and Death of Most Rev. I'rancis

of Tuam, author of Cambrensus Eversus,” (8vo., pp. 199). A new aund

pieces. His election as M.R.I.A. occurred in February, 1865, and the
firet edition of his ‘‘ 'ate and I'ortunes of Hugh O’Neill and Reory
O’Donel’’ appeared in 1868. (2nd ed., enlarged, 1870, 8vo., pp. xx.

+ 655 3rd ed. again enlarged, 1886.) 'This; work, by which he is best

known to the general reader which entitles him to rank as a painstak-
g and faithful historian was received with a chorus of praise. To
complete the Ulster trilogy he issued, without late, a wonder for him,
“The Life and Times of Hugh Roe O’Donnel”’—¢ dauntless Red
Hugh.” 1In 1869 appeared his last original work, which also neces-
sitated much laborious onquiry and careful sifting of material—** The
Rise and Fall of the Irish Iranciscan Monasteries,” 12mo., other
editions 1870 and 1872. ““ A wcholarly and delightful book,” said poor

J. . O’Donnell, in the introduction to his:*“ Memories of the Irish

During the last ten years of Father Meehan’s life hig literary work
consisted entirely of editing, with most careful notes, the works in

lieve his last work was the edi ting of Dr. Madden’s * L teriary Remains
of the United Irishmen,” 1887, from a parce. of MS. bought by the
publishers at the sale of the doctor’s library. Father Meehan passed
away amidst the regrets of rich and poor ony 14th March, 1890, and a
tablet to his memory has heen erected in the church in which he
officiated so long and faithfully.

IRISH LITERARY SOCIETY.

On 17th April Dr. J. S. Crone, J.P., entertained an overflowing
audience with an interesting Tea-table Talk on that brilliant writer,
wit and scholar, Dr. William Maginn, who was born in Marlborough
Court, Cork, in 1793—not ‘* Marlboro’s IFort,”” as the D.N.B. has it.
Educated under his father—one of the best known teachers of his time
—he entared T.C.D. at the age of 15, graduating B.A. three years later
and LL.D. in 1815. Returning to his native city he assisted his father
ard turned scribbler in ¢ Bolster’s Magazine,” nick-named ‘“ The Cork-
screw ' from the scanty remuneration awarded its contributors. The
@tablishment of ** Blackwood’s Magazme ” in 1817 afforded him a
wider field for the exercise of his abilities, and month after month he
poured forth in its pages a stream of mirth, mystification and erudition
under various pseudonyms, never before equalled in periodical litera-
ture. A vigit paid by him to Edinburgh introduced him personally—
after a practical joke played on Mr. Blackwood—to the gay band who
bhen carried on. “ Old Ilbony,” headed by Lockhart and Wilson, and
culminated in 2 acquaintance with Scott. He returned home, got mar-
ried, and armed with introductions from hig Edinburgh oronies he
launched on the precarious carcer of a man of letters in London in
1823. Ho had previously contributed to Jerdan’s ‘‘ Literary Gazette,’’
but his firgt engagement was on ‘‘ John Bull,”” then at the hey-day of
